text: The surgeon begins the procedure by conserving both adnexa, using a harmonic ace to cut the round ligament, fallopian tube, and utero-ovarian ligament.
The surgeon uses a bipolar device to control the adnexal vein and artery, ensuring hemostasis of these large vessels following the initial dissection with a harmonic device.
The surgeon places a bipolar device one centimeter outside the uterine cornea and uses it to cut through the large pedicle at the same distance from the uterine cornea.
The surgeon uses a harmonic ace to continue the dissection, freeing the adnexa completely from its uterine attachment after releasing the round ligament and utero-ovarian ligament.
The surgeon uses insufflation to facilitate dissection, opening both aspects of the broad ligament while progressing towards the bladder.
The surgeon cuts the posterior leaf of the broad ligament, effectively skeletonizing the entire uterine pedicle, preparing it for further dissection.
The surgeon performs dissection to gain better control over the right uterine artery and vein, ensuring precise manipulation and visibility of these vascular structures.
The surgeon applies bipolar energy directly to the uterine artery and vein, followed by the removal of the vesicovaginal peritoneum.
The surgeon carefully retracts the bladder cranially, avoiding damage due to previous C-section scars, while the anterior valve of the uterine manipulator is visible.
The surgeon clearly visualizes the anterior valve of the uterine manipulator. The next step involves cutting the round ligament using a harmonic scalpel, revealing a large adnexal vein nearby.
The surgeon uses a harmonic device to control the fallopian tube and applies bipolar energy to the utero-ovarian pedicle for hemostasis, ensuring a safe and bloodless section.
The surgeon uses energy devices to achieve a bloodless section, then proceeds to remove the left adnexa from its corneal attachment, cutting through the tissue without bleeding.

The surgeon uses the harmonic device through the suprapubic port to remove the peritoneum surrounding the uterine artery and vein. After skeletonizing the left uterine pedicle, the bipolar device is applied to control the large pedicle.
The surgeon uses the harmonic ace to cut a two-centimeter pedicle, demonstrating effective control and precision in the procedure.
The surgeon performs the same procedure on the opposite side, initially coagulating both pedicles before cutting the left pedicle to minimize bleeding on the residual pedicle.
The surgeon controls both the uterine artery and vein, then proceeds to cut the upper part of the uterosacral ligament to increase uterine mobility. The colpotomy is performed using a monopolar hook.

The surgeon uses a monopolar needle to perform a bloodless section, guided by the uterine manipulator. The same procedure is repeated on the right side, ensuring precise dissection and control of the tissue.
The surgeon performs a bloodless cut on the upper part of the right uterosacral ligament, followed by cutting the posterior aspect of the vagina using surgical instruments.

The surgeon uses a Chardonnay knife, a cold blade passed through a ten millimeter trocar, to perform a uterine hemissection. The uterus is carefully cut into two equal pieces for safe vaginal extraction, ensuring the bowel is not damaged during the procedure.
The surgeon performs an abdominal hemissection of the uterus, minimizing the risk of vaginal trauma. Both halves of the uterus are then removed through vaginal traction.
The surgeon begins the vaginal closure using a large 64 needle with a V-cremant stitch, opting for a rudder knot and interrupted sutures rather than a running suture.

The surgeon begins closing the vagina by suturing the left and right edges, ensuring the upper part of the uterosacral ligament is included in the stitches. The well-vascularized vaginal tissue is visible during this process.
The surgeon closes the median part of the vagina using a V-cremant stitch technique, ensuring the closure is secure and well-aligned with previous sutures.
The surgeon approaches the anterior aspect of the vagina and proceeds to the posterior aspect, performing intracorporeal knotting to achieve a secure closure of the vaginal cuff.
The surgeon uses an interrupted suture technique to completely close the vaginal cuff, ensuring each stitch is placed to minimize the risk of vaginal ischemia.
